raw.regions_all: Suriye; Irak; Mısır; Kuzey Afrika; İspanya; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir; Sind; Ermenistan; Kafkasya
raw.narrative_en: The Umayyad Caliphate transformed the Islamic state from a tribal republic into a centralized empire spanning the Mediterranean to Central Asia. The Damascus-based dynasty, founded by Mu'awiya, consolidated state identity under Abd al-Malik by making Arabic the official language and minting Islamic coinage. While conquests reached Spain and Sind, the mawali grievances and Ahl al-Bayt opposition deepened the regime's legitimacy crisis.

raw.parent_tribe: Quraysh - Hāshim (Hz. Peygamber'in amcası al-'Abbās'ın soyundan)
raw.regions_all: Irak; İran; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir (başlangıçta); Kahire (sonraki dönem)
raw.narrative_en: The Abbasids built the golden age of Islamic civilization and remained the symbolic center of the Sunni world for five centuries. They founded Baghdad, transforming it into the era's greatest intellectual and commercial hub where the Bayt al-Hikma translated Greek, Indian, and Persian knowledge into Arabic. The reigns of Harun al-Rashid and al-Ma'mun represented the zenith, while from the 10th century real power passed to military dynasties like the Buyids and Seljuks. The shadow caliphate in Cairo served a symbolic function, legitimizing Mamluk sultans.

raw.regions_all: İfrîkıye; Mısır; Güney Suriye; Filistin; Sicilya; Hicaz; Yemen (davet)
raw.narrative_en: The Fatimids were a dynasty founded on the Ismaili Shia caliphal claim that created Cairo as its capital, building an alternative Islamic center to the Abbasids. They founded al-Azhar, leaving one of the Islamic world's most enduring educational institutions; Druzism took shape under al-Hakim, while the Nizari-Musta'li split determined the future of the Ismaili movement.

raw.narrative_en: The Ayyubids were the dynasty founded by the Kurdish-origin Saladin after abolishing the Fatimid caliphate, recapturing Jerusalem from the Crusaders at Hattin. Their confederative structure uniting Egypt and Syria symbolized the resurgence of Sunni Islam, though this decentralization eventually led to their fragmentation.

raw.regions_all: Hicaz; Suriye; Filistin; Mısır; Irak; İran; Bahreyn; Umman; Yemen
raw.narrative_en: The Rashidun Caliphate represents the founding political order of the Islamic community after Prophet Muhammad's death, dismantling the Sasanian Empire entirely and expelling Byzantium from Syria and Egypt within three decades. Abu Bakr secured Arabian unity through the Ridda Wars, while Umar transformed the conquests into a systematic state apparatus. Uthman standardized the Quranic text, but the civil strife (fitna) under Ali created the defining political fracture in Islamic history.

raw.notes: Ismaili Shia caliphate; ancestral to current Aga Khan lineage

raw.regions_all: Horasan; İsfahan; Bağdat; Suriye; Kirmân; Mâverâünnehir (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Seljuqs represent one of the most decisive turning points in Islamic history and the founding force of the Turco-Islamic synthesis. Descending from the Qınıq clan of the Oghuz Turks, the Seljuqs defeated the Ghaznavids at the Battle of Dandanaqan (1040), dominated Khurasan, and within fifteen years took Būyid Baghdad, initiating the resurgence of Sunni Islam. Tughril Beg received the title 'Sultan of the East and West' from the Abbasid caliph, permanently institutionalizing the sultanate in Islamic politics. Alp Arslan's victory at Manzikert (1071) opened Anatolia to the Turco-Islamic world, while the era of Malik Shāh and his vizier Niẓām al-Mulk constituted one of the most brilliant administrative periods in Islamic history. The Niẓāmiyya madrasas institutionalized the Islamic educational system, building the intellectual infrastructure of Sunni orthodoxy. Branches established in Iraq, Kirman, Syria, and Anatolia under the Great Seljuq umbrella redrew the political map of the Islamic world.

raw.region_primary: Anadolu → Güneydoğu Avrupa → Ortadoğu → Kuzey Afrika
raw.regions_all: Söğüt/Bursa (erken); İstanbul; Anadolu; Balkanlar; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Irak; Kuzey Afrika; Kırım
raw.narrative_en: The Ottomans established the longest-lasting and largest empire in Islamic history, spanning three continents over six centuries. Rising from a small Anatolian frontier principality to world power, the Ottomans became heirs to the Roman Empire through the conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the political and religious leader of the Islamic world by assuming the caliphate. Reaching the zenith of law, architecture, and military power under Süleyman the Magnificent, the empire created a distinctive civilizational model through Sinan's masterpieces, the kapıkulu system, and the multi-religious millet structure. This dynasty of 36 sultans exited the historical stage after World War I defeat through the National Struggle and the proclamation of the Republic.

raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Hicaz; Kuzey Irak kısmen; Kilikya; Nübya; Kıbrıs (haraçgüzar)
raw.narrative_en: The Mamluks were a dynasty founded by slave-origin military elites that halted the Mongol invasion at Ayn Jalut and completely expelled the Crusaders from Syria. Throughout the Bahri and Burji periods, they positioned Egypt and Syria as the political and cultural center of the Islamic world, developing a unique system where succession passed not from father to son but to the strongest commander.

The Tulunids were the first local dynasty to achieve de facto independence from the Abbasids in Egypt and Syria. The Ibn Tulun Mosque, built by Ahmad ibn Tulun of Turkic origin, still stands in Cairo as one of the oldest and best-preserved examples of Islamic architecture.

raw.regions_all: Musul; Cezire (Kuzey Mezopotamya); Halep; Kuzey Suriye
raw.narrative_en: The Hamdanids ruled in two branches at Mosul and Aleppo, with Sayf al-Dawla transforming his Aleppo court into the era's most brilliant intellectual center. By patronizing figures like al-Mutanabbi and al-Farabi, they left an enduring mark on Islamic cultural history while maintaining the jihad tradition on the Byzantine frontier.

The Shihab Emirs governed Mount Lebanon after the Ma'nids, becoming one of the most influential local actors in Ottoman provincial politics under Bashir II. Ruling amid Druze-Maronite balances, they witnessed Western powers' regional interventions in the early 19th century.

raw.regions_all: Şūf; Lübnan Dağı; geniş dönemde Palmira'ya kadar Suriye
raw.narrative_en: The Ma'nid Emirs of Lebanon governed southern Lebanon as a Druze feudal lordship during the Ottoman period, laying the early foundations of Lebanese national identity through the visionary leadership of Fakhr al-Din II. His diplomatic contacts with Italy and independence efforts challenging the Ottomans constitute a turning point in Lebanese history.

The Ikhshidids were the second Turkic dynasty to rule Egypt after the Tulunids. The de facto regency of Kafur, a Nubian eunuch slave, stands as a remarkable example of social mobility in Islamic history; Egypt experienced a brief cultural flourishing under his patronage of the poet al-Mutanabbi.

The Muhammad Ali Dynasty was founded by the Albanian-origin military commander who launched Westernization and modernization in Ottoman Egypt. While radically reforming the army, education, and agriculture, the Suez Canal project elevated Egypt to the center of global trade. The dynasty maintained nominal sovereignty even under British occupation until the 1952 Free Officers coup.

raw.notes: Slave-soldier sultanate; Bahri and Burji dynasties

names.en: Chief Dā'īs of the Nizārī Ismā'īlī

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Chief Dā'īs of the Nizārī Ismā'īlīs or Assassins in Syria
raw.successor: Memlûk Baybars'ın tâbiiyeti (671/1273'e kadar kaleler teslim)
raw.narrative_en: The Syrian Nizari Ismailis formed the Syrian branch of the Alamut-based movement, operating from mountain fortresses as a militant sectarian organization. Under Rashid al-Din Sinan's leadership, they pursued an independent policy between Crusader and Muslim forces, influencing the political balance through assassination tactics.

The Mirdasids rose from the Kilab Bedouin tribe, governing Aleppo for half a century as a small but strategically important dynasty. They represented Northern Syria's transitional period, navigating balance between Fatimid, Byzantine, and Seljuk pressures.

raw.notes: Bedouin tribal confederation; Auda Abu Tayi famous
